---
title: "Quest 1: Variables - Your Starter Toolkit"
subtitle: "Learn How Rust Stores Values Safely"
format:
  live-html:
    code-tools: true
---

::: {.quest-badge}
🎒 QUEST 1 | Difficulty: Beginner | Time: 8 minutes
:::

::: {.tip-box}
**💻 Practice Options:**
- 🦀 [Open Rust Playground](https://play.rust-lang.org/)
- 📓 [Open Notebook](../jupyterlite/content/01-variables.ipynb)
- 📥 [Download Notebook](../files/lessons/01-variables.ipynb)
:::

## 📖 Introduction

Welcome to Rust! A variable is a named place to store a value. Rust starts with **immutable** variables, which means values do not change unless you explicitly allow it.

## 💡 Core Idea

```rust
fn main() {
    let player_name = "Ari";
    let score = 10;
    println!("{} has {} points", player_name, score);
}
```

To change a variable, add `mut`:

```rust
fn main() {
    let mut hearts = 3;
    hearts += 1;
    println!("Hearts: {}", hearts);
}
```

## 👨‍💻 Example

```rust
fn main() {
    let name = "Nova";
    let mut coins = 0;

    coins += 5;
    coins += 10;

    println!("{} collected {} coins", name, coins);
}
```

## 🎯 Gentle Challenge

Create variables for:
1. Your name
2. Your starting energy (number)
3. Whether you have a key (`true` or `false`)

Then increase energy by 2 and print all values.

## ✅ One Possible Solution

```rust
fn main() {
    let name = "Sam";
    let mut energy = 5;
    let has_key = false;

    energy += 2;

    println!("Name: {}", name);
    println!("Energy: {}", energy);
    println!("Has key: {}", has_key);
}
```

::: {.quest-complete}
### ✨ Quest 1 Complete
You learned `let`, `mut`, and simple printing with `println!`.
Next: [Quest 2: Data Types](02-data-types.qmd)
:::
